summary.intent: Migrate the web-ui JavaScript codebase from plain ES modules with HTM tagged templates to TypeScript with Preact JSX, adding a Vite build pipeline for type safety and better developer tooling.
summary.outcome: Full migration completed: 142 TypeScript files (~42.6K LoC) converted, Vite build pipeline set up, Rust integration updated, 0 TypeScript errors, Vite build succeeds (479 modules, 688KB bundle), and Rust workspace compiles clean.

repo: The project uses HTM (html tagged templates) with Preact instead of JSX — this is what made TypeScript migration non-trivial since TS cannot type-check inside template literals.
JS is served as native ES modules with zero build tooling — Rust embeds assets via include_dir! macro, so adding Vite required updating the asset pipeline and HTML templates.
Shiki syntax highlighting was previously loaded from CDN (shiki_url template variable in Rust templates.rs); after migration it's bundled by Vite, eliminating the CDN dependency and CSP allowlisting.
sendChat was called in websocket.js without being imported — a pre-existing bug/global reference that had to be resolved by triggering the send button click event instead.
The project has 3 separate entry points: main (chat app), login, and onboarding — each needed its own Vite entry and HTML template update.
54 locale files existed that needed mechanical rename + type annotation.
Shiki language grammars are code-split into ~620 individual chunks by Vite due to dynamic imports.

code.finding: sendChat is called but never imported — pre-existing bug where the function was referenced as if global. Fixed in TS version by triggering chatSendBtn.click() instead.
Contained shiki_url template variable and CDN CSP allowlisting — both removed after Vite bundles Shiki directly.
Subagent incorrectly added sendChat import from ChatPage — this was a hallucinated import that didn't exist in the original codebase.

workflow: Subagents hit output token limits on large files (5656-line page-settings.js) — large files need to be split into parts and handled by separate agents.
Subagents hallucinate imports that don't exist in the original codebase — all agent-generated imports need verification against the original source.
Running tsc --noEmit after Vite build is important: Vite strips types and succeeds even with 236 type errors; only tsc catches them.
Parallel subagent batches work well for mechanical conversions (stores, utilities, locale files) but require careful coordination for files with cross-dependencies.
Rate limits (token limits) were hit twice during the session, requiring 'proceed' prompts to resume work — long autonomous migrations need rate limit tolerance built in.

friction: Hit Claude rate/token limits twice mid-migration, requiring user intervention to continue despite being asked to work autonomously.
Largest file (page-settings.js, 5656 lines) exceeded subagent output token limit and had to be split into two parts and handled manually.
Subagents used kebab-case import paths for PascalCase component files (e.g. '../components/session-header' instead of '../components/SessionHeader'), causing build failures.
Subagents left stale htm/preact imports in some files that had been converted to JSX, requiring a grep-and-fix pass.
Check script for remaining unconverted files had a bug (false positives) — had to manually verify which files were truly missing.
236 TypeScript errors remained after mechanical conversion — required a dedicated bulk-fix pass before reaching 0 errors.
open_items: The old JS source files in crates/web/src/assets/js/ still exist alongside the new TS sources in crates/web/ui/src/ — the old files should be deleted once the migration is validated in production.

Vite build output (crates/web/src/assets/dist/) needs to be integrated into the Rust include_dir! asset embedding — the transcript shows HTML templates updated but doesn't confirm the Rust asset serving was fully wired up.
Pre-existing gateway warnings in Rust compilation were noted but not addressed.
No automated tests were run to verify runtime behavior after the migration — E2E Playwright tests were explicitly deferred as 'optional, low value'.
The service worker (sw.ts) was converted but caching strategies may need updating for the new bundled asset paths vs. the old per-file ES module paths.
Bundle size (688KB, 186KB gzipped) was not evaluated against a baseline — potential for optimization via code splitting of non-Shiki code.
